title: Death by chocolate cake
subtitle: A deep chocolate layer cake with ganache and chocolate buttercream
excerpt: Death by chocolate cake layers a dense cocoa sponge with dark chocolate ganache and a smooth chocolate buttercream. The sponge gets its depth from both cocoa powder and melted dark chocolate, and hot coffee in the batter intensifies both without adding a coffee flavour. Three layers, fully covered in ganache, finished with a buttercream swirl.
sourceNotes: The double-chocolate cake method, using both cocoa powder and melted chocolate for depth, is well established in the British and American home-baking tradition. The use of hot coffee or hot water to bloom cocoa powder appears in American recipes from the 1930s and has been standard in British baking books since the 1980s. The ganache and buttercream finishing is part of the established British celebration-cake canon. This recipe is synthesised from those sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: The naked sponge layers freeze well individually wrapped for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature before filling and frosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponges one day ahead. Wrap well and store at room temperature. Fill and frost on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; fan gives a flatter, more level sponge

glossaryTerms.definition: Ganache is a mixture of melted chocolate and hot cream. The standard ratio for a pourable glaze is equal weights of chocolate and cream. For a thicker ganache that firms to a truffle-like consistency, use twice as much chocolate as cream. Stir from the centre outward to form a smooth emulsion; do not whisk or it will trap air and look matte.

Pouring hot liquid over cocoa powder before mixing dissolves the cocoa solids and releases their full flavour. A tablespoon of instant coffee in the hot water or milk intensifies chocolate flavour further without making the cake taste of coffee. The technique is standard in American and British chocolate cakes.

content.text: This cake earns its name through layering: a dark cocoa sponge deepened with melted chocolate and hot coffee, filled with chocolate buttercream, then glazed with a dark
. The sponge uses the creaming method for a close, moist crumb. The coffee in the batter is not detectable as a flavour; it
and makes the chocolate taste more itself.
Make three 20 cm sponge layers for the classic look, or two deeper 23 cm layers for a simpler assembly. The bake time is the same either way.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Grease three 20 cm round tins and line the bases with baking paper. Sift together the 280 g flour, 60 g cocoa, 2 tsp baking powder, 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda and half a teaspoon salt. Beat the 150 g softened butter and 300 g caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time, then mix in the 100 g melted chocolate and the dissolved espresso. Fold in the flour mixture and 200 ml milk in alternating additions, beginning and ending with flour. Divide between the three tins and bake for 28 to 32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the sponges spring back when pressed lightly. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and leave until completely cold.

Put the 200 g chopped dark chocolate in a bowl. Heat the 200 ml double cream in a small pan until it just comes to the boil, then pour over the chocolate. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir from the centre outward until smooth. Leave at room temperature for 30 minutes until it thickens to a pourable but not runny consistency.

Beat the 200 g softened butter until very pale. Sift in the 300 g icing sugar and 40 g cocoa in two additions, beating well after each. Add the 2 tbsp milk and beat for 3 minutes until light and spreadable.

Place one sponge layer on a board or plate. Spread with about a third of the buttercream. Repeat with the second layer and buttercream, then place the third sponge on top. Smooth any remaining buttercream around the sides as a thin crumb coat. Chill for 30 minutes. Pour the thickened ganache over the top and let it drip down the sides naturally. Leave at room temperature for at least 30 minutes to set before slicing.

items.problem: Ganache is too runny to hold on the cake
items.solution: The cream was too hot or the ganache was poured while still very warm. Leave it to cool and thicken at room temperature until it coats the back of a spoon. If the kitchen is warm, 15 minutes in the fridge will help.
The butter and sugar were not beaten long enough (aim for pale and noticeably fluffy), or the eggs were added too quickly causing the mixture to curdle. Curdled batter produces a heavy crumb.

items.problem: Layers split or crumble when assembled
items.solution: The sponges were not completely cold before filling. Hot sponge melts the buttercream and makes the layers slide. Always cool completely on a wire rack before assembling.
